headline: Electric Buses Cut Pollution in Oxford by 10%
meta_description: Oxford's shift to electric buses brought a 10% drop in nitrogen dioxide across the city and up to 24% on major routes.
summary_short: Oxford's fleet of 159 electric buses has dramatically cut air pollution, proving that transit electrification benefits entire communities.
body:
Oxford, England has seen measurable improvements in air quality following the introduction of 159 electric buses into service. Citywide, nitrogen dioxide levels dropped by 10 percent—a significant reduction for a single intervention. On the city's busiest bus routes, the improvement was even more dramatic, with some locations recording decreases of up to 24 percent.

Nitrogen dioxide is a harmful pollutant linked to respiratory health problems, particularly in children and older adults. The sharp decline demonstrates the direct, real-world impact of replacing diesel-powered vehicles with electric alternatives in urban centers.

This success offers a template for other cities considering similar transitions. By switching public transit to electric power, municipalities can improve air quality rapidly and measurably, benefiting the health of everyone who lives and works in the city. Oxford's experience shows that large-scale environmental change is possible when cities commit to cleaner transportation.
